author: @markhughes7927
text:
8:08
I discovered some years ago that you can syncopate 18 identical elements to achieve a ‘syncopated cubeoctahedron’
All elements have quarter diameter incisions made from corners towards the centre of the thickness of the material used and - of course - each alters from its neighbour by 90 degrees.
I found that there were four ‘classic’ elements possible: a circular element, its inscribed square, its circumscribing square, and a cruciform cutting away most of the quadrants of the circle and leaving the cross behind.
The augmented square incises from the mid edge as with your example and presents a full cube in its achieved form but one having eight regular cubicles contained within.
A fifth ‘hybrid’ form is achieved by connecting the extremities of the cruci-form and giving it a perimeter with triangular spaces between that perimeter and the centre.
This ‘mule’ form gives versatility because it may carry the four classic forms when the 18-element figure is extended in series - which it can be made to do in the same dimensions as a cube - and different reactive configurations result.
The finished form - if using the simple ‘inscribed square’ - projects 12 elements for further associations to be made but at off-setness of 60 degrees - very wind-catching I would think.
No need for sticking or gluing: the cubeoctahedron itself is scarcely a structure - more a system of relations according to a knowledgeable commentator - but its syncopated form is like the rock of Gibraltar by comparison.
5 - 8 - 5 - 8 - 5 - 8 etc. but 5 - 8 - 5 to start with if any wishes to attempt the exercise - thus three strata which mutually interlock or ‘syncopate’.

author: @melellington4892
text: To those who question how this could possibly spin since the wind would be equally applied to both sides: you need to do a little research on the Magnus effect.  There are sailing ships that use vertical rotating cylinders, such as the Flettner rotors, to obtain energy from the wind for propulsion.  Obviously, a vertical cylinder standing upright in the wind is symmetrical and yet it can spin due to the Magnus effect.  It is a long known property of fluid dynamics.  Have you ever seen water flow down a toilet or sink?  Why would it spin when a naive understanding of physics would assume the water would sink equally from all directions?  Sorry…fluid dynamics, i.e. aero and hydro dynamics, are not so simple.

author: @darrellpidgeon6440
text: An interesting shape I never thought of this way.  The interior planes have a basic geometry where they are ninety degrees apart from each other.  If made of metal, a radar beam would be reflected straight back to it's source no matter the structure's orientation.  The exterior planes act as partial enclosures.  It makes me wonder what the wind pressure is doing inside the ninety-degree cavities, as well as on the outside surfaces.  Especially when the orientation changes on the axis.  What if the wind is not isolated to a narrow blow, but hits the entire exposed structure.  What behavior then?

author: @ark-543associates4
text:
Two more useful ideas for utilizing wind energy are: 

1. - wind-powered direct HEAT production people like us Alaskan's would really be able to use - ever heard of the wind-chill-temperature, the faster the wind when it's cold the colder you feel which also means the faster the wind the more directly produced heat in can generate (thru turbulence creation is just one option) (the Aussie's years ago invented a highly efficient wind powered HOT-water heater which is just a horizontal disk with lots of holes in it enclosed within a tight envelope - that could easily power a hot water heat system)  but just directly heated air (like in a forced air heater) would be such a blessing for some many cold dwellers and after all one of the problems of air compressors is getting RID of the heat they generate.  Wind driven heater would seem simple to make once the most efficient driver is figured out.

2. - would be wind generated compressed-air because older LARGE propane tanks are not hard to find make great air batteries and compressed air could care less (mostly) if speeds are widely varied way less problems generating compressed air then fussy electrical generation not counting the HIGH price and shorter life of easily damaged from poor maintenance batteries.  Possibly using wind effects in a fluid to generate compressed air like the great efficiency of the old Trompe method of compressed air generation thru falling aerated water.  

I agree with many other comentor's here- - one of my top favorite channels.   Grateful to have your contagious inspirational enthusiasm !!
